title: About
text: Hello my name is Zoe! Here is my blog, which consists of interesting data about sports and some of my interests. I am a junior here at the University of Nebraska at Lincoln studying sports media and broadcasting.

title: Is Husker Volleyball Built On Depth Or Just One Star?
text:
In college volleyball, being efficient on offense is a big part of winning, especially for a team like Nebraska, who is always competing at a high level. One of the best ways to measure this is through hitting percentage, which takes into account kills, errors, and total attempts to show how effective a player is when attacking. Lets first break it down by comparing each player’s hitting percentage side by side. By laying out the numbers this way, it becomes easier to see who stands out as the most efficient attacker and how big the gap is between players. It also helps show whether efficiency is spread pretty evenly across the team or if a few players are clearly leading the offense.


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(ggbeeswarm)

husker_data <- read_csv("husker_volleyball.csv")

husker_data <- husker_data |>
  rename(
    Player = player,
    Kills = K,
    Errors = E,
    Attempts = TA,
    HittingPct = PCT
  )

husker_data <- husker_data |>
  select(-`#`)

ggplot(husker_data, aes(x = reorder(Player, HittingPct), y = HittingPct)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Nebraska’s Most Efficient Attackers Stand Out from the Rest",
    
    x = "Player",
    y = "Hitting Percentage",
    caption = "Source: Nebraska Athletics | By: Zoe Leggett"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









Most of Nebraska’s players are clustered within a similar range of hitting percentages, which highlights the team’s overall consistency and depth on offense. It’s also important to note that these numbers represent cumulative performance across the entire season, rather than single-game highs. While some players may have had standout matches with unusually high hitting percentages, this data reflects sustained efficiency over time. Still, Manaia Ogbechie stands out from the group, leading the team in hitting percentage and emerging as the most efficient attacker. This chart shows who is most efficient, but it doesn’t account for how often each player is actually getting the ball. The next graph looks at attack attempts alongside hitting percentage to see if players with more opportunities are able to stay just as efficient.


Code
husker_data <- husker_data |>
  filter(Attempts > 0)

library(ggrepel)

ggplot(husker_data, aes(x = Attempts, y = HittingPct)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  geom_text_repel(aes(label = Player)) +
  labs(
    title = "Efficiency Drops as Offensive Workload Increases",
    
    x = "Total Attack Attempts",
    y = "Hitting Percentage",
    caption = "Source: Nebraska Athletics | By: Zoe Leggett"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









This graph shows how efficiency changes as players take on a larger role in the offense. While there isn’t a dramatic trend, higher-volume players tend to fall into a more average efficiency range, likely because they are taking more difficult swings. Meanwhile, players with fewer attempts, like Manaia Ogbechie, are able to maintain higher efficiency, highlighting how role and opportunity impact offensive performance. The scatterplot shows how efficiency changes depending on a player’s role, but it doesn’t fully capture how consistent the team is overall. This final graph looks at the full distribution of hitting percentages to show how Nebraska stacks up across the entire roster.


Code
library(ggbeeswarm)

ggplot(husker_data, aes(x = reorder(Player, HittingPct), y = HittingPct)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = Player == "Manaia Ogbechie"), size = 3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = mean(husker_data$HittingPct), linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "gray40", "TRUE" = "red")) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Nebraska’s Depth Drives Offensive Efficiency",
    
    x = "Hitting Percentage",
    y = "Player",
    caption = "Source: Nebraska Athletics | By: Zoe Leggett"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")









This final graph highlights how consistent Nebraska’s offense is across the roster. Most players are grouped around the team average, showing that the Huskers rely on multiple efficient attackers rather than just one dominant scorer. That level of balance makes the offense more difficult to defend and helps explain the program’s overall success. At the end of the day, Manaia Ogbechie is clearly Nebraska’s most efficient attacker based on her hitting percentage over the full season. Even with different roles and workloads, the team’s overall consistency is what really stands out. That depth is a big reason why Nebraska continues to be one of the top programs in college volleyball.

title: The Story of Half-Court Shots in College Basketball
text:
Half-court shots are some of the most dramatic and memorable moments in college basketball, often happening in desperate, last-second situations.
Before looking at when half-court shots occur or whether they are successful, it is important to first understand just how rare they are. Instead of looking at all shots, I focused on the longest attempts in the dataset and compared how often different teams attempt them. This helps show whether half-court shots are a regular part of any team’s offense or simply rare, last-second situations.


Code
library(tidyverse)

library(hoopR)

pbp_data <- load_mbb_pbp(seasons = 2025)

half_court_team <- pbp_data %>%
  filter(points_attempted > 0) %>%   # REAL SHOTS ONLY
  filter(!is.na(coordinate_x) & !is.na(coordinate_y)) %>%
  mutate(
    shot_distance = sqrt(coordinate_x^2 + coordinate_y^2),
    team = ifelse(team_id == home_team_id, home_team_name, away_team_name)
  ) %>%
  filter(shot_distance >= quantile(shot_distance, 0.9998, na.rm = TRUE))

team_counts <- half_court_team %>%
  group_by(team) %>%
  summarise(
    attempts = n()
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(attempts)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 10)

ggplot(team_counts, aes(x = reorder(team, attempts), y = attempts)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Half-Court Shots are a Last Resort",
    x = "Team",
    y = "Half-Court Shot Attempts"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold")
  )









The difference is clear: while teams take dozens of normal shots in a single game, half-court attempts occur at a rate close to zero. This shows that half-court shots are not just uncommon, but extremely rare events. They are not part of regular offensive strategy, but instead represent rare, last-second attempts when players have no other option.
Half-court shots may be rare, but they are also not random. Instead, they tend to occur in very specific moments within a game. To explore this, I examined when long-distance shots are taken, focusing on the final seconds of each half.




Code
half_court_time <- pbp_data %>%
  filter(shooting_play == 1) %>%
  filter(!is.na(coordinate_x) & !is.na(coordinate_y)) %>%
  mutate(
    shot_distance = sqrt(coordinate_x^2 + coordinate_y^2),
    seconds_remaining = clock_minutes * 60 + clock_seconds
  ) %>%
  filter(shot_distance >= 35)   

half_court_time <- half_court_time %>%
  filter(seconds_remaining <= 30)

ggplot(half_court_time, aes(x = seconds_remaining)) +
  geom_bar() +
  labs(
    title = "Long-Distance Shots Happen at the End of Halves",
    x = "Seconds Remaining in Half",
    y = "Number of Shots"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold")
  )









The results show a clear pattern: long-distance shots overwhelmingly occur in the final seconds of a half. Rather than being part of normal offensive strategy, these attempts are typically last-second efforts when players have no better option before time expires.
While extreme long-distance shots are rare, some do go in. This raises an interesting question: are certain teams more successful than others at making these shots, or are they simply random occurrences?

Because the hoopR coordinate system does not directly correspond to exact court distances, I define half-court-style shots as the longest attempts in the dataset.


Code
team_makes <- pbp_data %>%
  filter(points_attempted > 0) %>%
  filter(!is.na(coordinate_x) & !is.na(coordinate_y)) %>%
  mutate(
    shot_distance = sqrt(coordinate_x^2 + coordinate_y^2),
    result = ifelse(scoring_play == TRUE, 1, 0),
    team = ifelse(team_id == home_team_id, home_team_name, away_team_name)
  ) %>%
  filter(result == 1) %>%   
  filter(shot_distance >= quantile(shot_distance, 0.999, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  group_by(team) %>%
  summarise(makes = n()) %>%
  arrange(desc(makes)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 10)

ggplot(team_makes, aes(x = reorder(team, makes), y = makes)) +
  geom_col(fill = "darkred") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Teams With the Most Made Half-Court-Style Shots",
    x = "Team",
    y = "Made Shots"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold")
  )









Even the teams with the most makes only have a few successful attempts. This shows that these shots are not something teams can rely on, but instead are rare and unpredictable plays.
Extreme long-distance shots show up in highlights and feel like big moments, but the numbers tell a different story. Teams don’t take them often, and when they do, it’s almost always at the end of a half when there aren’t many other options. Even the teams that have made the most of these shots only have a few, which makes it clear there’s no real skill or consistency behind them.
So what looks like a special play is really just a low-percentage attempt that occasionally goes in. Most of the time, it doesn’t.
